class_schedule.topic: The Digital Transformation of International Relations
learning_objectives: Analyze how digital technology has reshaped diplomacy, conflict, and governance
Identify the key actors in cyberspace: states, corporations, civil society, hackers
Evaluate the concept of cyber sovereignty
readings: Hague, An Open and Secure Cyberspace (speech)

lecture_notes.introduction: Digital technology has created a new domain of international relations: cyberspace. Its governance is the great unresolved question of 21st-century diplomacy.
key_concepts: Cyberspace as the fifth domain (after land, sea, air, space)
Multi-stakeholder governance: states, companies, technical community, civil society
Cyber sovereignty: China and Russia's vision of state-controlled internet
Internet freedom: Western vision of an open, interoperable internet
The governance gap: norms lag behind technology
lecture_notes.demonstration: Stakeholder mapping: who governs what in cyberspace?

review.value: The principle that states have the right to govern their national cyberspace, including content filtering, data localization, and internet shutdowns -- advanced by China and Russia against Western multi-stakeholder models
The model where governments, private sector, civil society, and technical community share governance responsibilities for the internet, exemplified by ICANN and the Internet Governance Forum

class_schedule.topic: Digital Diplomacy: Social Media, Data, and the Modern Embassy
learning_objectives: Analyze how foreign ministries use digital tools for diplomatic engagement
Evaluate the risks and opportunities of Twitter/X diplomacy
Assess how data analytics are transforming consular and diplomatic work
readings: Manor, The Digitalization of Public Diplomacy Ch. 1-2 (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Diplomacy is being digitized. From social media engagement to AI-powered consular services, technology is transforming how states interact with each other and with foreign publics.
key_concepts: Digital diplomacy: using digital tools for diplomatic objectives
Twitter/X diplomacy: direct leader-to-public communication across borders
Data diplomacy: using data analytics for foreign policy intelligence
Virtual embassies and digital consular services
Risks: miscommunication, escalation, information security
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing the social media strategies of five foreign ministries
setup_requirements: Foreign ministry social media accounts analysis

review.value: The use of social media platforms by heads of state and diplomats for direct international communication, bypassing traditional diplomatic channels -- pioneered by leaders from Trump to Modi to Zelenskyy
The use of big data analytics in foreign policy: from predicting political instability using social media sentiment to managing consular caseloads through AI

class_schedule.topic: Cyber Conflict: Operations, Norms, and Deterrence
learning_objectives: Categorize types of cyber operations: espionage, sabotage, subversion
Evaluate the application of international law to cyber conflict
Analyze why traditional deterrence frameworks struggle in cyberspace
readings: Rid, Cyber War Will Not Take Place Ch. 1-3 (excerpt)
UN GGE 2021 Report on Responsible State Behavior in Cyberspace (summary)

lecture_notes.introduction: Cyber operations occupy a gray zone between peace and war. Developing norms and deterrence strategies for this domain is one of the most urgent challenges in international security.
key_concepts: Spectrum of cyber operations: espionage, disruption, sabotage
Notable operations: Stuxnet, SolarWinds, NotPetya, Colonial Pipeline
International law in cyberspace: sovereignty, non-intervention, use of force thresholds
UN GGE and OEWG: competing norm-setting processes
Deterrence challenges: attribution, proportionality, signaling
lecture_notes.demonstration: Cyber incident analysis: classifying operations and evaluating legal and policy responses

review.value: The US-Israeli cyber weapon that sabotaged Iranian nuclear centrifuges (2010), the first known cyber operation to cause physical damage -- crossing the threshold from espionage to sabotage
The 2015 and 2021 consensus reports establishing voluntary norms for responsible state behavior in cyberspace, including not targeting critical infrastructure and cooperating on cybercrime

learning_objectives: Analyze the institutions governing the global internet: ICANN, IETF, IGF
Evaluate debates over internet freedom, content moderation, and platform regulation
Assess digital rights: privacy, expression, access
readings: DeNardis, The Global War for Internet Governance Ch. 1-2 (excerpt)
UNHRC Resolution on the Promotion, Protection and Enjoyment of Human Rights on the Internet

lecture_notes.introduction: The internet's governance was designed for a technical community, but it is now a political battleground where sovereignty, rights, and economic power collide.
key_concepts: Internet governance institutions: ICANN (domains), IETF (protocols), IGF (dialogue)
Net neutrality: treating all internet traffic equally
Content moderation: platform responsibility vs. free expression
Data governance: GDPR, data localization, cross-border data flows
Digital rights: privacy, expression, access, and the digital divide
lecture_notes.demonstration: Policy debate: should platforms be liable for user content?
setup_requirements: Regulatory comparison: EU DSA vs. US Section 230

review.value: The Internet Corporation for Assigned Names and Numbers: the multi-stakeholder organization managing the Domain Name System, critical for internet functionality and increasingly contested geopolitically
The 2022 EU regulation requiring platforms to address illegal content, provide transparency on algorithms, and protect user rights, representing the most comprehensive platform regulation globally

class_schedule.topic: AI Governance and Emerging Technology Diplomacy
learning_objectives: Analyze the international governance landscape for artificial intelligence
Evaluate national AI strategies and their geopolitical implications
Assess the challenge of governing technologies that advance faster than regulation
readings: Kissinger, Schmidt & Huttenlocher, The Age of AI Ch. 1-2 (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: AI is the most transformative technology since the internet. Its governance will determine whether it serves humanity broadly or concentrates power dangerously.
key_concepts: AI governance landscape: OECD Principles, EU AI Act, US Executive Order, China's approach
The AI arms race: US-China competition in chips, models, and talent
Autonomous weapons: the campaign to ban killer robots
AI safety: alignment, existential risk, and international cooperation
Technology diplomacy: export controls, chip restrictions, and tech alliances
lecture_notes.demonstration: Institutional design exercise: designing an international AI governance institution

review.value: The world's first comprehensive AI regulation, categorizing AI systems by risk level (unacceptable, high, limited, minimal) and imposing requirements for transparency, accountability, and human oversight
Weapons systems that can select and engage targets without meaningful human control, subject to ongoing debates in the UN Convention on Certain Conventional Weapons about a potential ban

class_schedule.topic: The Future of Digital Governance: Scenarios and Strategies
learning_objectives: Assess competing visions for the future of cyberspace governance
Evaluate the geopolitics of technology standards
Develop a strategic framework for digital foreign policy
readings: Atlantic Council, Declaration for the Future of the Internet (2022)

lecture_notes.introduction: The future of the internet and digital technology governance is being decided now. The choices made will shape international relations for decades.
key_concepts: Three visions: open internet, sovereign internet, commercial internet
Weaponized interdependence: how network chokepoints become tools of coercion
Technology standards as geopolitical competition: 5G, AI, quantum
The Brussels effect in digital governance: EU as global regulator
Digital foreign policy: integrating technology into diplomatic strategy
lecture_notes.demonstration: Strategy exercise: developing a digital foreign policy strategy for a mid-sized democracy

review.value: Farrell and Newman's concept that the centralized structure of global networks (financial, internet, supply chain) creates chokepoints that powerful states can exploit for coercion and surveillance
The geopolitical contest over who sets technical standards for 5G, AI, quantum computing, and other emerging technologies, with implications for economic competitiveness, security, and values

questions.question: Compare the Western 'multi-stakeholder' model of internet governance with the Chinese/Russian 'cyber sovereignty' model. What values does each prioritize?
questions.answer: The multi-stakeholder model involves governments, private sector, civil society, and the technical community sharing governance of the internet. It prioritizes openness, interoperability, free expression, innovation, and bottom-up standard-setting, exemplified by institutions like ICANN and the IGF. The cyber sovereignty model asserts that states have the right to control cyberspace within their borders, including filtering content, localizing data, and regulating platforms. It prioritizes state security, social stability, and national control over information. China enforces this through the Great Firewall; Russia through its sovereign internet law. The fundamental tension is between individual rights and innovation (multi-stakeholder) versus state control and social order (sovereignty). In practice, even Western democracies are moving toward more regulation (EU DSA, GDPR), suggesting some convergence, though the fundamental values remain in tension.

questions.question: Why is deterrence more difficult in cyberspace than in the nuclear domain? Identify two specific challenges.
questions.answer: Deterrence is harder in cyberspace for several reasons. First, the attribution problem: unlike nuclear weapons, where the origin of an attack is immediately obvious (missile trajectory, nuclear signature), cyberattacks can be routed through multiple countries, use false flags, and exploit the anonymity of the internet. Without reliable attribution, the defender cannot credibly threaten retaliation against a specific attacker. Second, the difficulty of signaling capability and resolve: nuclear deterrence relies on visible demonstrations of capability (tests, parades) and clear communication of red lines. In cyberspace, capabilities are inherently secret (revealing them neutralizes them), making it impossible to demonstrate deterrent capability without using it. Additionally, there is no agreement on what constitutes a 'cyber attack' equivalent to an armed attack, making proportional response calculation extremely difficult.

questions.question: What is 'weaponized interdependence' and how does it apply to the digital domain? Provide one example.
questions.answer: Weaponized interdependence (Farrell and Newman) is the concept that the hub-and-spoke structure of global networks creates chokepoints that powerful states can exploit for coercion and surveillance. Unlike the liberal assumption that interdependence promotes cooperation, weaponized interdependence shows how network centralization creates asymmetric power. In the digital domain, examples include: the US leveraging its control over the SWIFT financial messaging system to enforce sanctions (Iran, Russia) because most global transactions flow through US-connected networks; US control over semiconductor supply chains to restrict China's access to advanced chips; and the ability of US intelligence agencies to conduct surveillance through US-headquartered tech companies and internet infrastructure through which global traffic flows. The concept explains why states are pursuing digital sovereignty and supply chain diversification.

questions.question: Why is governing autonomous weapons particularly challenging from an international law perspective?
questions.answer: Governing autonomous weapons is challenging because: (1) Definition problem: there is no agreed definition of what constitutes an 'autonomous weapon' -- the spectrum from human-controlled to fully autonomous is continuous, making a clear prohibition target difficult. (2) Verification: unlike nuclear weapons (which are large and detectable), autonomy is a software capability that cannot be easily verified through inspections. (3) Dual-use: the same AI technology powers both civilian applications (self-driving cars) and weapons, making export controls and bans difficult without impeding legitimate innovation. (4) IHL application: existing law requires human judgment for proportionality and distinction assessments in combat -- but it's unclear whether algorithmic decision-making can satisfy these requirements, and different interpretations exist. (5) Strategic incentives: major military powers (US, China, Russia) see strategic advantages in autonomous weapons and resist binding restrictions that might constrain their militaries.

major_assignments.title: Digital Governance Policy Paper
major_assignments.description: Select a digital governance challenge (cybersecurity, AI regulation, platform governance, digital rights, or internet governance) and produce a comprehensive policy analysis with recommendations for a specific government or international organization.
objectives: Analyze a digital governance challenge through multiple lenses
Communicate technical policy to non-technical audiences

criteria: Thorough understanding of the technical dimensions

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on cyber governance, digital diplomacy, and technology policy

participation.description: Engagement in simulations, tabletop exercises, and policy debates

major_assignments.description: Digital governance policy paper and presentation

midterm_examination.description: Exam on cyber operations, internet governance, and digital diplomacy

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam with scenario-based digital policy analysis

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance required. Tabletop exercise absences cannot be made up.
course_policies.collaboration: Simulation teams collaborate. Written work individually authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work original. Responsible disclosure of any vulnerabilities discovered required.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to cybersecurity databases, policy think tank publications, and digital governance resources.
